notes.anchor: This part to you in my own hand
notes.note: Cicero signals that what follows is autograph rather than dictated to a secretary, marking it as confidential. The private matter is the painful business of his estranged second wife, Publilia.

notes.anchor: Publilia has written to me that her mother
notes.note: Publilia was Cicero's young second wife, whom he had married after divorcing Terentia and from whom he became estranged after Tullia's death. Here she and her family press for a reconciliation Cicero is determined to avoid, even by leaving the country.

notes.anchor: within the rents from the Argiletum and Aventine
notes.note: Cicero owned tenement blocks (insulae) in the Argiletum district and on the Aventine hill at Rome; their rents are to fund his son's allowance while studying at Athens. The Aventine has no separate entity record in this corpus.

notes.anchor: neither Bibulus nor Acidinus nor Messalla, who I hear will be at Athens
notes.note: Three young Roman nobles --- L. Calpurnius Bibulus, a Manlius Acidinus, and the future orator M. Valerius Messalla Corvinus --- were going to Athens to study at the same time as Cicero's son. Cicero uses their expected spending as the benchmark for his own son's budget.
